children.name: Indicator
children.description: Drably plumaged birds with a remarkable behavioural adaptation — they guide humans and honey badgers to bee nests, then feed on the exposed wax and larvae after the nest is broken open. True to their scientific name, they are living indicators of wild bee colonies. They are brood parasites, laying their eggs in the nests of barbets, woodpeckers, and other cavity-nesting birds.

children.name: Indicator indicator
children.description: The best-known honeyguide and the species most famous for guiding humans to bee nests. It ranges across sub-Saharan Africa in savanna and woodland habitats. It has a distinctive chattering call that it uses to attract the attention of potential guiding partners, then flies a short distance and calls again to lead the way.

children.name: Indicator minor
children.description: A smaller counterpart of the Greater Honeyguide, found across sub-Saharan Africa in forests and woodlands. It has a less prominent guiding behaviour than the Greater Honeyguide but still engages in the practice. It is also a brood parasite, with a particular preference for host nests of barbets and tinkerbirds.

children.name: Indicator variegatus
children.description: A forest-dwelling honeyguide found from West Africa through the Congo Basin to East Africa and south to Angola and Zambia. It has olive-brown upperparts and a distinctive scaly pattern on the throat and breast. It is less frequently observed than other honeyguides due to its more secretive forest habits.

children.name: Prodotiscus
children.description: Small, slender honeyguides with a fine, sharply pointed bill that resembles a sunbird's more than a typical honeyguide's. They are the least known and most poorly studied of the honeyguides, with a preference for forest and woodland habitats.

children.name: Prodotiscus zambesiae
children.description: A small, slender honeyguide of savanna and miombo woodlands in East and southern Africa. It has olive-green upperparts and a pale grey belly, with a long, fine bill that it uses to probe flowers for insects. The least known of the honeyguides, its breeding biology and guiding behaviour remain poorly documented.
